content_en:
Ibn Qudamah opens the substantive legal content of Al-Mughni with the topic of taharah (purification), following the universal convention of Islamic legal manuals: worship begins with ritual purity. His treatment is characteristically exhaustive — no major point of dispute among the schools is left unaddressed, and every position is supported by its textual evidence.

The Hanbali school divides water into categories relevant to purification. Absolutely pure (tahir mutahhir) water is natural water unchanged by external impurities — rainwater, river water, well water, spring water, ocean water. The Prophet confirmed: "The sea — its water is pure (tahur) and its dead animals are lawful" (Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi). Water in this category is effective for purification. Water that has been altered in one of its three properties (color, taste, or smell) by a pure substance — such as flowers or a small amount of soap — remains usable according to most scholars if the alteration is slight. Water altered by najasah (ritual impurity) is impure and may not be used for purification.

The Hanbali school holds that a large body of water (two qullahs or more — approximately 190 liters) does not become impure unless its properties actually change, based on the hadith: "If water reaches two qullahs, it does not carry filth" (Abu Dawud, Ibn Majah). This is the position of the Shafi'i school as well. The Hanafi school applies a different standard: water is considered impure only if the impurity is visible or the water is small (less than ten by ten cubits).

Wudu in the Hanbali school has six obligatory acts: washing the face, washing the hands and forearms to the elbows, wiping the entire head, washing the feet to the ankles, maintaining the order (tartib) of these acts, and performing them continuously (muwalah) without undue interruption. The requirement to wipe the entire head is based on the Quranic text (5:6), and the Hanbali school does not accept wiping a portion of the head as sufficient — unlike the Hanafi school, which considers wiping a quarter of the head sufficient.

The Hanbali school gives extensive attention to the rules of wiping over leather socks (khuffayn) and footwear, based on the many authentic hadiths confirming the Prophet's practice. The permissible duration is one day and night for residents and three days and nights for travelers. Wiping is nullified by the same acts that nullify wudu, and also by removing the socks or by the expiration of the time limit.

Ghusl (major purification) in the Hanbali school requires: intention, washing the entire body including the hair roots, and rinsing the mouth and nostrils (which the Hanbali school considers obligatory within ghusl, unlike the Shafi'i school, which considers them only recommended in ghusl). The school also holds that the ghusl of a person in a state of janabah who enters water with the intention of purification is valid even without a specific sequential method, as long as the whole body is wetted.

Ibn Qudamah discusses in detail the sources of major ritual impurity (janabah): ejaculation, sexual intercourse, menstruation, and post-natal bleeding. He addresses the scholarly disputes over whether janabah occurs from intercourse even without ejaculation — the majority position (including Hanbali) being that it does, based on the hadith: "When the two circumcised parts meet, ghusl becomes obligatory" (Muslim).

The depth of Al-Mughni's coverage of taharah reflects the foundational importance of this topic — and the author's conviction that every Muslim deserves access to the full scholarly discussion behind the rulings they observe.
